text: The surgeon performs a direct trocar entry into a large ovarian cyst to debulk it. Using bipolar cautery and scissors, a cut is made over the anti-macentric or anti-hylar border of the ovary, opening it into two folds of tissue. The bipolar settings are at their lowest to minimize damage to the ovarian cortex. The ovarian tissue is visualized as it unfolds, facilitating the subsequent cystectomy.

The surgeon initiates the cystectomy by applying gentle traction and counter traction using needle holders, one gripping the ovarian cortex and the other the cyst wall. The dissection proceeds meticulously, with bipolar coagulation used to manage tiny capillaries and venous oozing, ensuring minimal bleeding as the correct plane is maintained.
The surgeon utilizes three assistant ports to perform an ovarian cystectomy, with increased bleeding observed near the hilar region of the cyst wall.

The surgical field shows the opened bed of the ovarian cyst with minimal bleeding due to meticulous dissection. The surgeon begins dissecting the large para ovarian cyst, with its collapsed wall clearly visible.

The surgeon completes the para ovarian cystectomy using a needle holder for steady traction and bipolars or graspers for gentle counter traction, allowing the mesosalpingial tissue to slide over the cyst smoothly. The ovarian cyst is located in the left gutter, and the para-ovarian cyst is in the right gutter for clear identification.

The surgeon introduces an endobag through the ten mm lateral port and unfolds it towards the pelvis. The large cystectomy specimen is gently placed into the endobag. Using two needle holders, the ovarian cyst is held at both ends and scissors are used to cut through the center, forming elongated ribbons to facilitate retrieval through the abdominal wall.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of three ports during cystectomy, with two lateral ports providing traction and a central port used for scissors to form ribbons from the cyst tissue. The unedited procedure for para ovarian cystectomy begins, showcasing the ribbonizing technique inside the endobag.

The surgeon has transformed the cyst specimen into a single long stripe using the ribbonizing technique. As the specimen is removed from the abdomen through the port, it emerges as a continuous strip of tissue. Sutures are placed over the surface of the ovary to keep it crumpled within the pouch of Douglas.
